text_a: Zakariya Essabar, a Moroccan citizen, moved to Germany in February 1997 and to Hamburg in 1998, where he studied medical technology. Essabar turned extremist fairly suddenly, probably in 1999, and reportedly pressured one acquaintance with physical force to become more religious, grow a beard, and compel his wife to convert to Islam. Essabar's parents were said to have made repeated but unsuccessful efforts to sway him from this lifestyle. text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer set up his own spot (had to be married to do that). Folks think now that Durer probably didn’t cut the woodblocks himself; a pro craftsman would’ve handled that. text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer set up his own shop (you had to be married to do this). Over the next five years, his style started mixing in Italian influences with them Northern styles. Durer's daddy passed in 1502, and his mama in 1513. The best stuff he made early on in the shop were his woodcut prints, mostly about religion, but he had some everyday scenes too like The Men's Bath House (around 1496). These were bigger and cut finer than most German woodcuts before, and they were way more complex and balanced in how they were put together. Folks now think it's unlikely that Durer cut any of the woodblocks himself; a specialist craftsman probably did that work. But, his training in Wolgemut's studio, where they made a lot of carved and painted altarpieces and both designed and cut woodblocks for woodcuts, clearly taught him a lot about what you could do with the technique, and how to work with block cutters. Durer either drew his design right on the woodblock, or he stuck a paper drawing to the block. Either way, his drawings got ruined when they cut the block.

text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer started his own shop (you had to be married to do that). Over the next five years, his style started mixing in Italian vibes with them Northern styles. Durer's daddy passed in 1502, and his mama in 1513. The best stuff he did early in the shop were his woodcut prints, mostly about religion, but he had some everyday scenes too like The Men's Bath House (around 1496). These were bigger and more detailed than most German woodcuts before, and way more complex and balanced in how they was put together. Folks think now that Durer probably ain’t cut none of them woodblocks himself; a specialist craftsman woulda done that. But, his training in Wolgemut's studio, where they made a lot of carved and painted altarpieces and both designed and cut woodblocks for woodcut, for sure taught him a lot about what you could do with the technique, and how to work with them block cutters. Durer either sketched his design straight on the woodblock, or stuck a paper drawing on the block. Either way, his drawings got wrecked when they cut the block.
text_b: Durer was known back in the early days of his workshop for his religious works, but where did he get his experience working on religious art? Wit da monks

text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer set up his own spot (you had to be married to do that). Over the next five years, his style started mixing in Italian vibes with the Northern styles he knew. Durer's dad passed away in 1502, and his mom in 1513. The best stuff he made early on in his workshop were his woodcut prints, mostly about religious stuff, but he threw in some everyday scenes too like The Men's Bath House (around 1496). These cuts were bigger and cleaner than most German woodcuts before them, and they were way more complex and balanced in how they were set up. Folks now think it's unlikely Durer did any of the actual woodcutting himself; that job was probably done by someone who specialized in that. But the training he got in Wolgemut's studio, where they made a lot of carved and painted altarpieces and designed and cut woodblocks for prints, for sure gave him a deep understanding of what you could do with the technique, and how to collab with the block cutters. Durer either sketched his designs right on the woodblock, or he glued a paper drawing on it. Either way,
text_b: Which studio did Durer learn in? Durer learned in Wolgemut's studio

text_a: When he got back to Nuremberg in 1495, Durer set up his own spot (had to be married to do that). These were bigger and cut nicer than most German woodcuts before, and way more complex and balanced in layout. Folks think it's unlikely that Durer cut any of them woodblocks himself; that job was probably done by a specialist craftsman. But his training in Wolgemut's studio, where they made a bunch of carved and painted altarpieces and both designed and cut woodblocks for woodcut, clearly taught him a lot 'bout what you could do with the technique, and how to work with block cutters. text_a: When Durer got back to Nuremberg in 1495, he set up his own shop (had to be married to do that). Over the next five years, his art started mixxing Italian styles with Northern styles. Durer's dad passed away in 1502, and his mom in 1513. The best stuff he made early in the shop were his woodcut prints, mostly about religion, but also some regular scenes like The Men's Bath House (around 1496). These cuts were bigger and cleaner than most German woodcuts before, and they were more complex and balanced. People now think Durer probably didn't cut the woodblocks himself; a specialist would've done that. But, working in Wolgemut's studio, where they made a lot of carved and painted altarpieces and designed and cut woodblocks for prints, clearly taught him a lot about the craft and how to collaborate with block cutters. Durer either drew his designs straight on the woodblock, or stuck a paper drawing to the block. Either way, his drawings got ruined when they cut the block.
